iei.title: Intelligence Efficiency Index — Units of Intelligence per Unit of Energy
rows: IEI tracks the most important structural variable in markets right now: how much intelligence (AI compute capability) we get for each unit of energy spent. Every metric in this bar measures a different pressure point on that ratio.

The thesis: for the first time in history, intelligence-on-silicon is operating ~6 orders of magnitude faster than carbon-based (biological) intelligence — and chip efficiency keeps doubling. Whoever owns the energy supply for that compute owns the productivity gain.

What it bull-cases: uranium / nuclear (the power source hyperscalers are signing PPAs for), copper (every electric motor, data-center cooling, grid expansion), AI semiconductors (the efficiency frontier itself), and grid infrastructure (the bottleneck).
What it bear-cases: any business model that hasn't adapted to AI-driven productivity gains. Pure labor-cost businesses lose to compute-augmented competitors.
Why this matters: traditional macro metrics (CPI, GDP, employment) miss the AI capex wave entirely. IEI is the closest thing to a real-time gauge of the AI-energy thesis.

iei-score.title: IEI Composite Score (0–100)
rows: Single number combining chip efficiency gains, hyperscaler nuclear pipeline, Jevons paradox intensity, and grid stress.
0–30: AI-energy thesis weak — efficiency gains slowing, infrastructure builds stalled.
30–60: moderate — thesis intact but no fresh catalysts.
60–100: strong structural demand pressure building — best environment for uranium / copper / semis / grid plays.
Higher is BETTER for the thesis — means more compute demand chasing constrained energy supply, which is the bull case for everything physical that supports the AI buildout.

iei-chip.title: Chip — Latest AI Generation + Efficiency Gain
rows: The current frontier AI chip + its efficiency improvement (FLOPS per watt) vs the previous generation.

Why it matters: each generation resets the cost-per-intelligence curve. A 3× efficiency jump means 3× more useful AI for the same electricity bill — which simultaneously makes more applications viable (Jevons) AND lets data centers do more with less power per unit.
Reference: Moore's Law gave ~40% efficiency gains per generation. AI accelerators are running at 2–5× per generation. That's unprecedented in industrial history.

Sustained >100% per gen = AI capability per dollar growing faster than any prior technology curve. Massively bullish for NVDA, AMD, AVGO, TSM, ASML, plus every business that scales on AI productivity.

iei-nuclear.title: Nuclear — Confirmed Hyperscaler PPAs
rows: Power Purchase Agreements signed by hyperscalers (Amazon, Microsoft, Google, Oracle, Meta) specifically for AI data-center electricity. Measured in gigawatts of committed capacity.
Why this is the real data: these are SIGNED contracts with dollar amounts and specific reactors / sites — not press-release projections. Real committed infrastructure spend.

What rising GW signals: hyperscalers themselves are saying their AI buildouts need baseload nuclear power that the existing grid can't provide. This is the strongest fundamental signal for uranium miners (CCJ, NXE, UEC, DNN) and SMR developers (OKLO, SMR, NNE, LEU).
Why now: renewables alone can't supply 24/7/365 data-center loads. Nuclear is the only zero-carbon baseload. The pipeline started in 2024 and is accelerating.

iei-bio.title: Bio Gap — Silicon vs Biological Intelligence Efficiency
rows: How many TIMES more energy-efficient the human brain is per unit of intelligence vs the best current AI chip. A shrinking gap means silicon is catching up to biology — and silicon never sleeps, can be replicated infinitely, and scales linearly with power.
Today: the gap is ~10,000× (the brain runs on 20 watts; equivalent AI capability burns megawatts).
Trajectory: at the current rate of chip-efficiency improvement, hardware reaches biological parity in ~12–14 years. After that, AI cognition becomes vastly cheaper than human cognition per task.
Implication for investors: the closer that gap gets, the more economically transformative AI becomes. Power demand for AI compute keeps rising because intelligence-per-dollar keeps falling.

iei-jevons.title: Jevons Paradox — Efficiency Gains Increase Total Consumption
rows: Named for William Jevons (1865): when something gets more efficient, total consumption RISES — not falls — because new uses unlock faster than the savings accumulate.

Why it matters here: every time AI chips get more efficient, total AI compute demand grows faster than the efficiency saves. Result: ever-increasing aggregate power consumption for AI, despite per-chip efficiency gains.
ACTIVE = current efficiency curve is being out-paced by demand growth. The bull case for nuclear, copper, grid infrastructure.
If it ever flips DORMANT (efficiency exceeds demand) — that would be the bear signal. Hasn't happened in any decade since electricity was invented. AI is the most-Jevons technology ever measured.

sci.title: Silicon Cognition Index — How Much Intelligence the World Has Access To
rows: IEI (purple bar) measures intelligence-efficiency — units of intelligence per unit of energy. SCI (this bar) measures the OUTPUT — how much silicon-based intelligence the world has access to, and how fast it's growing.

The framing: carbon-based intelligence (humans) has been measured for centuries — IQ tests, productivity per worker, GDP per capita. Silicon intelligence operates ~6 orders of magnitude faster, doesn't sleep, scales linearly with power, and can be replicated infinitely. SCI is the gauge nobody else surfaces.

Why this matters for your portfolio: if frontier capability flattens for 6+ months while AI capex keeps rising, that's the bear signal — your AI / nuclear / copper holdings are exposed. If capability keeps doubling every ~6 months, the thesis is alive and every dip is structural.

What the metrics measure: frontier model benchmark score (general reasoning capability), cost per million tokens (the price of intelligence), capability doubling rate, training compute scale, silicon-vs-biology efficiency gap, daily inference scale across the major providers.
Refresh cadence: monthly. Sources: published benchmark papers, public API pricing pages, industry-disclosed training-compute estimates.

sci-mmlu.title: Frontier Capability — MMLU Benchmark
rows: MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) is the most-cited benchmark for general AI reasoning. Tests 57 subjects from US history to formal logic to medicine. % = correct answers out of multiple choice questions.
Reference points: Random guessing = 25%. Average human = ~35%. Expert human = ~89%. Frontier AI today = 92%+.
What it tells you: the headline number for "how smart is AI right now?". When this score keeps rising, AI-exposed businesses keep getting more valuable per dollar of compute spent.

What's left: MMLU is approaching ceiling. Newer benchmarks (GPQA, ARC-AGI, FrontierMath) measure capabilities MMLU can't — and frontier models still score 25-60% on those. The capability curve isn't done.

sci-cost.title: Cost per Million Tokens — The Price of Intelligence
rows: How much it costs to run frontier AI on 1 million tokens of text (~750,000 words). Blended retail API pricing for the leading model.
Trajectory: dropped from ~\$60/million in 2020 (GPT-3) to ~\$3/million today. That's 20× cheaper while capability roughly doubled.

Why this is the bull case: falling cost × rising capability = exponential growth in the number of viable AI use cases. Every halving of cost unlocks a wave of applications that weren't economically possible at the old price.

The Jevons connection: cheaper intelligence doesn't lead to less spending on AI — it leads to more, because new applications come online faster than the cost savings accumulate. Confirmed bull for chip demand, power demand, infrastructure.

sci-doubling.title: Capability Doubling Rate — Faster than Moore's Law
rows: Months between benchmark-equivalent capability levels. AI capability has been doubling every ~6 months.
Reference: Moore's Law (transistor count) doubled every ~24 months. AI capability is moving 4× faster than the fastest tech-progress curve in history.

What sustains it: three multiplicative compounding curves — chip efficiency (the IEI angle), training compute scale, and algorithmic improvements. Each contributes ~10× per generation; together they multiply.
When to worry: if the doubling rate slows to >18 months, that's the signal the curve is bending. So far every published frontier model has continued the trajectory.

sci-compute.title: Training Compute Scale — FLOPS for the Biggest Model
rows: Total floating-point operations used to train the largest publicly-disclosed AI model. Best proxy for "how big is the biggest brain we're building".
Trajectory: 3×10²³ FLOPS (GPT-3, 2020) → 1.5×10²⁶ FLOPS today. 500× bigger in 6 years.

What this requires: billions of dollars in GPU capex, gigawatts of power for the data center, copper for the interconnects, water for cooling, real estate for the buildings. This is the physical-economy driver behind the AI buildout.
Why subscribers care: every uranium, copper, semiconductor, and grid-infrastructure position in the portfolio is leveraged to this curve continuing. SCI is the gauge that tells you whether it is.

sci-bio.title: Silicon-vs-Biology Efficiency Gap
rows: Orders of magnitude (powers of 10) that the human brain is more energy-efficient per unit of intelligence vs the best current AI chip. ~4 OOM today = the brain is ~10,000× more efficient.
The trajectory: the gap halves every ~2 years at the current chip-efficiency rate. At that pace, hardware reaches biological parity around 2040.

Implication after parity: AI cognition becomes vastly cheaper than human cognition for any task that fits in an LLM's context. The economic value of "knowledge work" hours starts compressing toward the cost of running silicon — which keeps falling.
Critically: this is the gap AT EQUAL CAPABILITY. Silicon is already FASTER, more PARALLEL, more REPLICABLE, and NEVER TIRED. Parity in efficiency just removes the last remaining biological advantage.

sci-inference.title: Daily Inference Scale — Tokens Served / Day
rows: Roughly how many tokens of text the major API providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Meta, plus open-source on hyperscaler GPUs) process per day combined. Order-of-magnitude estimate — providers don't publish exact numbers.
YoY growth: ~280% per year. Demand is growing faster than capacity expansion.
What it bull-cases: NVDA + AMD inference chips, every hyperscaler's revenue, data center REITs, power infrastructure, semiconductor equipment (ASML, AMAT, LRCX).
Where the bottleneck is: not silicon — power. This is why uranium signed PPAs spiked starting in 2024 (see Nuclear in the IEI bar above).

regime.title: Market Regime — System Operating State
rows: The system classifies the market into one of three operating regimes based on VIX. The regime controls which strategies run and how aggressively positions are sized.
TRENDING (VIX < 16.5): calm, directional market. Full strategy deployment. Momentum and breakout signals are most reliable.
CHOPPY (VIX 16.5–25): elevated uncertainty, range-bound action. Reduced position sizes. Only high-confidence signals pass through.
CRISIS (VIX > 25): risk-off panic mode. New entries paused. Capital preservation. Gold and defensive signals only.
Why it matters: The regime isn't just a label — it changes the system's posture. Wrong regime = wrong position size = wrong outcome.

vix.title: VIX — CBOE Volatility Index
rows: Measures the market's expected volatility over the next 30 days. Calculated from S&P 500 options pricing. Known as the "Fear Gauge."
VIX < 15: calm, low-fear environment. Trend-following and momentum work well. Risk assets broadly favoured.
VIX 15–25: moderate anxiety. Markets are uncertain. Range-bound strategies outperform. Reduce new entries.
VIX > 25: fear spike. Institutions are hedging hard. Safe havens (gold, bonds, USD) outperform everything else.
VIX > 40: once-per-cycle panic. Historically one of the best times to accumulate quality assets — but not before the peak.

sectors.title: Sector Performance — Daily Leaders & Laggards
rows: Tracks all 11 GICS sectors via their ETFs (XLK, XLE, XLF, XLV, etc.). Shows today's best and worst performers by % change.
Sector rotation: institutional money moves between sectors before it moves individual stocks. The leading sector today hints at where the big money is flowing.
Best sector: tailwind — signals in this sector carry more conviction this week. Institutional money is already moving in the same direction.
Worst sector: headwind — signals in this sector face selling pressure. Wait for stabilisation before acting on them.
Why it matters: A uranium buy signal during an energy sector tailwind week is worth more than the same signal in an energy headwind week. Context is everything.

mktsentiment.title: Market Sentiment — Fear & Greed Scale
rows: Derived from VIX. Translates raw volatility into an intuitive sentiment label. Extreme readings in either direction are historically contrarian signals.
Extreme Greed (VIX < 12): complacency. Everyone is already positioned long. Historically precedes corrections — reduce new longs, tighten stops.
Greed (VIX 12–16): positive but not dangerous. Good environment for momentum trades. Risk assets broadly favoured.
Neutral (VIX 16–20): mixed signals. Markets wrestling with uncertainty. Wait for directional clarity before sizing up.
Fear (VIX 20–25): money rotating to safety. Quality holds better than speculative names. Defensive signals in focus.
Extreme Fear (VIX > 25): panic. Historically a strong buy signal for quality assets — but only after the panic peaks, not during it.

cryptofg.title: Crypto Fear & Greed — Alternative.me Index
rows: Composite index (0–100) measuring sentiment across the entire crypto market. Built from Bitcoin volatility, trading volume, social media, surveys, BTC dominance, and Google Trends.
0–25 Extreme Fear: panic selling. Historically the strongest accumulation zones — patient buyers are rewarded 6–12 months later.
25–45 Fear: cautious market. Dip-buying emerging. Watch for reversal signals and on-chain accumulation.
46–55 Neutral: no strong directional bias. Market waiting for a macro or on-chain catalyst.
56–75 Greed: positive momentum. Trend strategies outperform. Watch for signs of overextension.
75–100 Extreme Greed: euphoria. Historically precedes sharp corrections. Take partial profits, tighten stops, reduce new entries.

cycle.title: Crypto Cycle — Market Phase
rows: The crypto market moves in 4-year cycles driven by Bitcoin halving events and macro liquidity. The cycle phase determines which assets are in play and how aggressively the system deploys capital.
BULL cycle: BTC above its 20-week EMA, volume expanding, altcoins outperforming. Full crypto strategy deployment — momentum and breakout setups active.
BEAR cycle: BTC below its 20-week EMA, volume contracting. Defensive posture — reduce exposure, rotate to stablecoins, wait for cycle bottom.
NEUTRAL: transition phase. Directional signal not yet confirmed. Reduced sizing until BULL or BEAR is confirmed.
Why it matters: The cycle phase is the most important variable in crypto — being in the wrong phase with full size is how fortunes are lost.

btc.title: Bitcoin (BTC)
rows: The original cryptocurrency and global crypto reserve asset. Every major move in BTC ripples through the entire market — altcoins amplify its direction.
BTC leads, alts follow: when BTC pumps, ETH and alts typically follow within hours to days. When BTC dumps, alts fall harder and faster.
BTC dominance rising: money flowing from alts into BTC — risk-off within crypto. Altcoins underperform.
BTC dominance falling: money rotating into alts — altcoin season. Higher-beta plays become more attractive.
Why it matters: BTC price and trend sets the crypto regime (BULL / BEAR / NEUTRAL). All other positions are sized relative to BTC's direction.

eth.title: Ethereum (ETH)
rows: The leading smart contract platform — the foundation of DeFi, NFTs, Layer 2s, and most major crypto applications. ETH is the "oil" that powers the Ethereum economy.
ETH/BTC ratio rising: altcoins outperforming Bitcoin — a leading indicator for broader alt season.
Staking yield: ~28% of all ETH is staked, earning ~3–4% yield. Locked supply creates structural buy pressure.
Layer 2 scaling: Arbitrum, Base, and Optimism route transactions through ETH, expanding the demand base without congesting mainnet.
Why it matters: ETH vs BTC performance tells us when to rotate from BTC into higher-beta alts — one of the most reliable cycle signals.

sol.title: Solana (SOL)
rows: High-performance Layer 1 — 65,000 transactions per second at fractions of a cent. Emerged as Ethereum's primary competitor for DeFi, consumer apps, and memecoins.
Speed and cost win: Visa uses Solana for stablecoin settlement. Major DeFi protocols, gaming, and consumer apps chose SOL for the throughput.
High beta: SOL typically moves 1.5–2× ETH in both directions. In bull markets it outperforms; in bear markets it falls harder.
Memecoin engine: the 2024 memecoin cycle ran almost entirely on Solana — retail onramp that drove DEX volume to record highs.
Why it matters: SOL is a high-conviction bet on "fast + cheap chain wins" vs Ethereum's security-first approach. Pure risk-on asset.

fet.title: Fetch.ai / ASI Alliance (FET)
rows: AI agent infrastructure token. FET merged with AGIX and OCEAN to form the ASI Alliance — the largest decentralized AI ecosystem by combined market cap.
AI agent thesis: if autonomous AI agents need to transact with each other, they need a crypto payment rail. FET is the leading candidate for that infrastructure.
ASI Alliance: combines AI data markets (OCEAN), AI compute (AGIX), and agent payments (FET) into a single ecosystem.
Why it matters: Pure-play exposure to the AI agent economy. If autonomous agents become real, FET is infrastructure — not application layer hype.

tao.title: Bittensor (TAO)
rows: Decentralized network for AI model training and compute. Miners contribute computing power and ML models; validators score quality; TAO rewards the best outputs.
Decentralized AI training: incentivizes open-source model production. GPU scarcity created by AI labs makes decentralized compute networks more valuable.
High beta: TAO historically moves 3–5× BTC during bull cycles. Extremely volatile — high conviction, small sizing.
Why it matters: The most direct bet on decentralized AI infrastructure. If AI compute decentralizes, TAO captures that value at the protocol layer.

rndr.title: Render Network (RNDR)
rows: Decentralized GPU render network — idle GPU owners monetize spare compute by selling it to artists, VFX studios, and AI developers. Partners include Apple and Adobe.
GPU scarcity play: Nvidia GPU demand from AI labs made idle GPU monetization valuable. RNDR connects supply with demand at scale.
Real revenue: unlike most crypto, RNDR has actual paying customers — render jobs are invoiced in RNDR tokens. Revenue drives real token demand.
Why it matters: AI infrastructure with enterprise adoption and real-world utility. One of the strongest AI + crypto crossover plays in the market.

hbar.title: Hedera (HBAR)
rows: Enterprise-grade distributed ledger governed by a council of ~39 major corporations: Google, IBM, Boeing, Deutsche Telekom, LG, and others. Built for institutional use.
Hashgraph consensus: technically superior to blockchain — 10,000+ TPS, 3–5 second finality, energy-efficient (5× more efficient than Visa). Used in real production systems.
CBDC infrastructure: multiple central bank digital currency pilots are running on Hedera. If a major economy launches a CBDC, Hedera becomes settlement infrastructure.
Why we watch it: low-liquidity, long-setup asset. When enterprise adoption news hits or CBDC pilot results publish, HBAR moves fast. We watch for the catalyst, not the drift.

sui.title: Sui (SUI)
rows: Next-generation Layer 1 built by ex-Meta engineers (Diem / Move language team). Designed from the ground up for high throughput and parallel transaction processing.
Move language: more secure than Solidity — assets are owned objects, not stored in contracts. Eliminates entire classes of exploits that have cost DeFi billions.
Parallel execution: most transactions on Sui process in parallel rather than sequentially — a fundamental throughput advantage over all existing L1s.
Why it matters: institutional-quality L1, early in its adoption curve. Direct competitor to Solana. Higher risk, higher potential — sized accordingly.

ada.title: Cardano (ADA)
rows: Peer-reviewed, academically rigorous blockchain built by Ethereum co-founder Charles Hoskinson. Every protocol change is formally verified before deployment.
Academic rigor: Haskell-based, formally verified, 100+ peer-reviewed research papers. Slow but arguably the most battle-tested smart contract platform.
Emerging markets thesis: Atala Prism (digital identity) deployed in Ethiopia, Kenya, and Tanzania. 1.7B unbanked people need financial infrastructure — ADA is building it.
Why it matters: long-cycle thesis. If the developing world uses crypto for digital identity and payments, ADA built the infrastructure. Lower beta, lower risk crypto position.

flux.title: Flux (FLUX) — Decentralized Cloud Computing
rows: Flux is the DePIN (Decentralized Physical Infrastructure) cloud platform — a global network of ~14,000+ nodes providing compute, storage, and networking as an alternative to AWS/Azure/GCP.
DePIN thesis: hardware operators earn FLUX by contributing real compute to the network. Anyone running a Flux node monetizes idle server capacity — the same model as Airbnb for cloud.
FluxOS: a decentralized operating system that runs Web3 apps across the node network. Apps deploy across multiple nodes automatically — no single point of failure.
Flux Labs: Flux's enterprise arm sells cloud capacity to AI and Web3 startups as a cheaper alternative to centralized cloud. Real revenue, real customers.
Why we watch it: pure-play DePIN infrastructure. If decentralized compute takes market share from centralized cloud, FLUX is one of the most direct plays on that shift.

nmr.title: Numerai (NMR) — AI Hedge Fund Intelligence Token
rows: Numerai is a hedge fund that crowdsources AI predictions from thousands of data scientists worldwide, then combines them into a single "meta-model" used to trade global equities.
Unique model: data scientists submit encrypted predictions (nobody sees the data) and stake NMR tokens on their own accuracy. Winners earn NMR; losers lose their stake.
Skin in the game: every prediction is backed by real capital. Scientists who outperform get paid. Those who submit noise get burned. Self-selecting for quality.
Erasure Protocol: NMR's underlying technology allows anyone to stake on the truth of any data — a general-purpose trustless data marketplace.
Why we watch it: where AI meets quantitative finance at the protocol level. NMR is both a governance token and a bet that decentralized AI prediction beats centralized research desks.

link.title: Chainlink (LINK) — Decentralized Oracle Network
rows: Chainlink is the dominant oracle network — the critical middleware that connects smart contracts to real-world data (prices, weather, sports results, election outcomes, API feeds).
The oracle problem: blockchains can't access external data natively. Every DeFi protocol, insurance contract, and prediction market needs trusted off-chain data. LINK is that bridge.
Market dominance: Chainlink secures $15+ trillion in transaction value and is integrated with every major DeFi protocol — Aave, Synthetix, dYdX, MakerDAO all depend on LINK feeds.
CCIP (Cross-Chain Interoperability Protocol): Chainlink is becoming the TCP/IP of blockchain — letting assets and data move seamlessly between chains. Swift and major banks are piloting CCIP.
Why we watch it: infrastructure that earns fees from every DeFi transaction. As on-chain finance grows, LINK's oracle revenue grows with it. Low-hype, high-utility position.

grt.title: The Graph (GRT) — Blockchain Data Indexing Protocol
rows: The Graph is the "Google of blockchain" — a decentralized indexing protocol that makes querying blockchain data fast, structured, and accessible. Without The Graph, reading on-chain data requires running your own node.
Subgraphs: developers publish indexing schemas (subgraphs) that organize blockchain data into queryable APIs. Uniswap, Aave, OpenSea, and hundreds of others use The Graph to power their apps.
Token economics: GRT is staked by indexers (who answer queries), curators (who signal quality subgraphs), and delegators (who back indexers). Fees flow through the network for every query answered.
Data Layer thesis: every application built on blockchain eventually needs fast, structured data. The Graph is the data indexing layer — equivalent to a database query engine for Web3.
Why we watch it: data infrastructure that grows with every new blockchain application. As on-chain activity increases, query volume and GRT fee revenue scale accordingly.

iotx.title: IoTeX (IOTX) — DePIN Infrastructure Chain
rows: IoTeX is the blockchain purpose-built for DePIN (Decentralized Physical Infrastructure Networks) — connecting physical devices (sensors, cameras, vehicles, energy meters) to the blockchain economy.
DePIN coordination layer: IoTeX provides the smart contracts, token standards, and identity infrastructure for physical hardware networks. Helium, DIMO, and dozens of others build on IoTeX.

W3bstream: IoTeX's off-chain compute layer that processes real-world device data before writing verified results to the blockchain. Handles the privacy and throughput requirements that on-chain can't.
MachineFi: IoTeX's vision of machine-to-machine finance — devices owning accounts, earning tokens, and paying for services autonomously. Early infrastructure for the autonomous economy.
Why we watch it: if DePIN becomes a $1T+ market (Goldman Sachs projection), IoTeX is the foundational coordination layer. High-risk, high-upside infrastructure play.

xrp.title: XRP (Ripple) — Institutional Payment Rails
rows: XRP is Ripple's bridge currency for cross-border bank-to-bank settlements. Over 100 financial institutions use RippleNet for international transfers — processing in 3–5 seconds vs SWIFT's 2–5 days.

SEC legal clarity: in 2023 a US federal court ruled XRP is NOT a security when sold to retail investors — the first major legal clarity for any crypto asset. Institutional confidence returned immediately.

On-Demand Liquidity (ODL): banks use XRP as a real-time bridge between illiquid currency pairs. Each ODL payment creates actual XRP buy/sell volume — demand tied to transaction throughput, not speculation.
XRPL CBDC sidechains: the XRP Ledger has built-in CBDC infrastructure being evaluated by multiple central banks. A major-nation CBDC launch on XRPL would be a major catalyst.
Why we watch it: largest non-speculative crypto use case in banking. Moves on Ripple partnership news, ETF filings, and CBDC pilot announcements. More institutional than retail-driven.

xlm.title: XLM (Stellar) — Emerging Market Payments
rows: Stellar is the open-source payment protocol focused on the 1.7 billion unbanked — fast, near-zero-cost transfers between any two currencies, built for individuals rather than banks.
IBM World Wire partnership: IBM used Stellar to process cross-border B2B payments across 50+ currencies for major financial institutions. Real enterprise adoption at the infrastructure layer.
CBDC leader: Stellar is actively building sovereign digital currency infrastructure for developing-world central banks. The Marshall Islands, Ukraine, and others are building on Stellar.

Anchor network: any licensed institution can issue stablecoins pegged to any currency on Stellar — a practical on-ramp for populations without bank access. MEnt for humanitarian payments and remittances.

Why we watch it: lower market cap than XRP = higher upside potential but more volatile. Catalyst-driven: moves on CBDC announcements, G20 financial-inclusion policy, and Stellar Development Foundation partnerships.

algo.title: ALGO (Algorand) — Institutional & Government Blockchain
rows: Algorand was founded by MIT cryptographer Silvio Micali and built for enterprise and government use — formally verified consensus, instant finality (< 4 seconds), and a carbon-negative footprint.

Government adoption: Italy's National Securities Commission uses Algorand for securities issuance. The Marshall Islands built its national digital currency (SOV) on Algorand. Australia piloted a digital bond settlement system on Algorand.
Pure Proof-of-Stake: unlike ETH's validator-heavy PoS, Algorand's consensus requires no staking lock-up — anyone can participate, blocks never fork, instant finality eliminates settlement risk.
Algorand Foundation grants: hundreds of millions in developer grants have built DeFi, RWA tokenization, and enterprise-grade applications on the network.

Why we watch it: the institutional and government adoption thesis. ALGO moves significantly on G20 country CBDC announcements, central bank pilot news, and real-world asset (RWA) tokenization deals. Less retail-driven, more policy-driven.

avax.title: AVAX (Avalanche) — EVM-Compatible High-Speed L1
rows: Avalanche is a Layer 1 platform built for speed and EVM compatibility — sub-second finality, 4,500+ TPS, and full Ethereum tooling compatibility. Developers can deploy Solidity smart contracts with zero changes.

Subnet architecture: institutions can launch their own custom blockchains (Subnets) with their own rules, validators, and tokenomics — all secured by Avalanche consensus. Visa and Amazon Web Services have both explored Avalanche Subnets.
Institutional push: Avalanche partnered with Deloitte for US disaster relief fund management, and multiple international banks have piloted CBDC issuance on Avalanche Subnets.
C-Chain / P-Chain / X-Chain: three interoperable chains handle contracts, staking, and asset exchange independently — reduces congestion that clogs single-chain networks.
Why we watch it: AVAX is a direct Ethereum competitor with institutional tailwinds. Moves on EVM adoption news, Subnet launches, and DeFi TVL flows. High beta to the Ethereum ecosystem.

dot.title: DOT (Polkadot) — Cross-Chain Interoperability
rows: Polkadot is a multichain network that connects sovereign blockchains (parachains) into one shared security umbrella. Built by Ethereum co-founder Gavin Wood, it solves the fragmentation problem — chains talk to each other natively.

Shared security model: parachains lease a slot on the Polkadot relay chain and inherit its validator set. New chains launch with institutional-grade security from day one instead of bootstrapping their own.
Parachain ecosystem: Moonbeam (EVM compatibility), Acala (DeFi hub), Phala (confidential computing), and 100+ parachains each specialise and interoperate via XCM (cross-consensus messaging).
JAM upgrade (2024-2025): the Join-Accumulate Machine replaces the relay chain with a programmable, highly parallel execution environment — designed to scale to millions of transactions per second.

Why we watch it: DOT is the interoperability thesis — if the future is multi-chain, Polkadot is the internet layer connecting them. Moves on parachain auction news, XCM adoption, and Ethereum bridge activity.

near.title: NEAR Protocol — The Chain for AI & Usability
rows: NEAR is a high-speed Layer 1 built for usability and AI integration. Human-readable account names, sub-second finality, and near-zero gas fees make it one of the most developer- and user-friendly blockchains available.
Sharding (Nightshade): NEAR horizontally partitions the network into shards that process transactions in parallel — designed to scale linearly as demand grows, unlike most single-chain networks.

Chain Abstraction: NEAR's Chain Signatures allow a single NEAR account to control wallets on Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana, and any other chain — no bridges, no multi-wallets. The endgame for cross-chain UX.

AI-native positioning: NEAR is one of the few chains actively building AI agent infrastructure. Autonomous AI agents can hold NEAR accounts, sign transactions, and interact with on-chain apps without human approval.

Why we watch it: as AI agents begin executing on-chain, NEAR's usability-first design and AI integrations position it early in a potential AI-crypto convergence. Moves on AI agent framework releases and developer adoption.

apt.title: APT (Aptos) — Move-Language High-Throughput L1
rows: Aptos is a Layer 1 blockchain built by ex-Meta engineers who created the Diem/Libra project. It uses the Move programming language — designed from scratch for safe, parallel asset management.

Move language safety: unlike Solidity (Ethereum's language), Move treats assets as first-class objects with formal ownership rules. This eliminates entire categories of exploits — reentrancy attacks, integer overflows, double-spends.
Block-STM parallel execution: Aptos processes independent transactions simultaneously using software transactional memory. Achieves 160,000+ TPS in testing — far beyond any EVM-compatible chain.

Enterprise & institutional push: Microsoft, Google Cloud, and Tether are active Aptos ecosystem partners. The network has attracted significant institutional capital for RWA (real-world asset) tokenization.

Why we watch it: Aptos represents the next architectural generation of smart contract platforms. High-performance L1 with institutional backing and a technically differentiated foundation. Moves on Move ecosystem adoption and institutional RWA deals.

ocean.title: OCEAN (Ocean Protocol) — Decentralized Data Marketplace
rows: Ocean Protocol is a decentralized data marketplace where data owners can monetize datasets while maintaining custody and control. Built to unlock the estimated $3T+ in data that is siloed and untapped.

Data NFTs + Data Tokens: every dataset is wrapped as a Data NFT (ownership) and gated by Data Tokens (access rights). This creates a permissioned, provenance-tracked, monetizable data economy on-chain.

Compute-to-Data: Ocean's signature feature — algorithms run against private data without the data ever leaving the owner's server. Enables AI training on sensitive data (healthcare, financial) without privacy violations.

AI data economy: as AI models require ever-larger, higher-quality training datasets, Ocean creates the infrastructure to price, discover, and transact on that data. Direct play on AI data supply chain.

Why we watch it: data is the new oil for AI — Ocean is the exchange. Moves on AI compute partnerships, healthcare data deals, and enterprise data tokenization announcements. Low market cap = high asymmetry.

cat-payments.title: Payments — Blockchain Settlement Rails
rows: Blockchain-based payment networks are replacing SWIFT and correspondent banking for cross-border settlements — faster (seconds vs days), cheaper (fractions of a cent vs $25–50 per wire), and 24/7.

Why it matters: global remittances exceed $700B per year. Cross-border bank settlements are a multi-trillion dollar market. Blockchain rails capture value through transaction volume and token burn mechanics.
What to watch: central bank digital currency (CBDC) announcements, banking partnership news, and ETF filing activity. Regulatory clarity is the primary catalyst for this category.
Dragonfly tracks XRP and XLM as the two most institutionally adopted payment protocols — both have active banking partnerships and measurable on-chain transaction volume.

cat-layer1.title: Layer 1+ — Alternative Smart Contract Platforms
rows: Layer 1 blockchains are the foundational settlement layers of the crypto economy. They compete to be the platform where developers build applications — each with different tradeoffs in speed, decentralization, and developer tooling.

Why multiple L1s exist: no single chain dominates across all use cases. Cardano (ADA) targets formal verification and governance. Algorand (ALGO) targets enterprise and government. Avalanche (AVAX) targets EVM compatibility. Polkadot (DOT) targets multi-chain interoperability. NEAR targets AI and usability. Aptos (APT) targets high-throughput enterprise.

How they move: L1 tokens are high-beta bets on ecosystem adoption. They outperform in bull cycles when capital rotates from BTC/ETH into alts, and underperform in bear cycles when risk-off flows dominate.
What to watch: developer activity (GitHub commits, new deployments), DeFi TVL flows, ecosystem grant programs, and any major application launches that drive actual on-chain usage.

cat-datalayer.title: Data Layer — Web3 Infrastructure for Data & Oracles
rows: The Data Layer category covers the infrastructure protocols that connect blockchain smart contracts to real-world data — oracles, indexers, IoT coordination layers, and data marketplaces.

Why it matters: smart contracts are isolated from the external world by design. They can't read stock prices, weather data, API results, or IoT sensor readings on their own. Data Layer protocols solve this — they are the nervous system of Web3.
LINK (Chainlink): the dominant oracle network. Feeds verified real-world data (prices, rates, events) to thousands of smart contracts across every major chain.
GRT (The Graph): the indexing layer. Makes blockchain data queryable — used by Uniswap, Aave, OpenSea, and hundreds of others to power their frontends.

IOTX (IoTeX): the DePIN coordination layer. Connects physical devices (sensors, vehicles, energy meters) to the blockchain economy. OCEAN (Ocean Protocol): the data marketplace. Enables data monetization while preserving privacy — critical infrastructure for AI training data.

cat-aiagents.title: AI Agents — Crypto for Autonomous Intelligence
rows: AI Agent tokens are cryptocurrencies built to power autonomous AI systems — networks where AI models own wallets, pay for compute, earn tokens, and make decisions without human approval.

FET (Fetch.ai): the Fetch.ai network enables AI agents to autonomously buy and sell services, data, and compute. Agents negotiate contracts and execute transactions on-chain with no human in the loop.
TAO (Bittensor): a decentralized AI training network where models compete to produce the best predictions. Miners train models; validators score outputs; the network pays the best performers in TAO.
RNDR (Render): decentralized GPU compute for AI rendering, training, and inference. Artists and AI developers rent idle GPU capacity from a global network — cheaper than AWS, scalable on demand.

Why it matters: as AI systems become more autonomous, they will need on-chain economic infrastructure — wallets, payments, compute marketplaces. These tokens are positioned to capture that value. High-risk, high-reward phase of the AI supercycle.

cat-watch.title: Watch List — Speculative & Emerging Plays
rows: The Watch section contains higher-risk, higher-potential tokens that show meaningful technological differentiation or narrative momentum — but have not yet fully proven their use case at scale.
HBAR (Hedera): enterprise-grade DLT backed by a governing council including Google, IBM, and Boeing. Unique hashgraph consensus is provably fair and asynchronous Byzantine Fault Tolerant.
SUI: next-generation L1 from ex-Meta/Diem engineers using Move language. Parallel execution, sub-second finality. Direct competitor to Solana and Aptos.
FLUX: decentralized cloud computing network — Web3 infrastructure for hosting, compute, and storage. An alternative to AWS built on a distributed node network.

NMR (Numeraire): the Numerai tournament token. Data scientists stake NMR on their models' predictions — earn or lose based on real-world performance. Unique mechanism aligning incentives in quant finance.

Why we watch them: these tokens have catalysts that could move them 5–20x in the right cycle. We size smaller and track momentum. When a Watch token consistently outperforms, it gets promoted to a core position.
